courses.title: Theory of Computing
courses.content: Finite Automata and Regular Languages: Deterministic and Nondeterministicfinite automata and their equivalence, Pumping lemma, the Myhill-Nerode Theorem; PushdownAutomata (PDA) and Context free languages (CFL): Context free grammars (CFG), normal forms,variants of pushdown automata, equivalence of PDA and CFG, pumping lemma for CFLs; TuringMachine (TM) and Computability: Variants of TM and their equivalence, Decidability andUndecidability, Halting problem, reductions, Rice's theorem; Complexity Theory: Time Complexity, P,NP, many-one reductions, NP-completeness, Cook-Levin theorem, Space Complexity.

courses.title: Operating Systems
courses.content: Operating systems basics: Evolution, history and design principles; Processes and threads: Kernel/user level operation, thread/process synchronization, system calls and APIs, interrupt handling, CPU scheduling; Memory management: virtual memory, address spaces and translations, segmentation and paging, TLBs and other caching techniques; Concurrency and synchronization: Race conditions, locks, semaphores, concurrent data structures; I/O systems and storage devices: Hard disk drives, solid state devices, file systems design principles and implementation, maintaining data integrity; Advanced topics: Virtualization, distributed systems.

courses.title: Computer Networks
courses.content: Introduction to Computer Networks: Layered Network Protocol Architectures: OSI and TCP/IP reference models. Overview of Telecommunications, Cellular networks & Internet Architecture; Physical and Link Layers: Basics of communications; Delay, Loss and Throughput. Physical media types and their important bandwidth and bit-error-rate characteristics; Wired and Wireless media including copper cables, optical fiber and wireless. Framing; Error control: Bit-parity, CRC and Hamming Codes; Medium Access Control (MAC): Shared media systems; Bus, Star and Ring topologies; TDMA, FDMA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, Ethernet and IEEE 802.3; IEEE 802.11 including CSMA/CA protocols; Shared and Switched Ethernet; Introduce protocols such as ARP, RARP and ICMP; Network Layer: Design Principles: Protocols, Addressing and Routing. Study of Internet Protocol (IP) suite; Hierarchical network architectures; IPv4 and IPv6 addressing and headers; Routing protocols: distance-vector and link-state approaches; Interior and Exterior Gateway Protocol concepts; Routing Algorithms including Dijkstra's algorithm and distributed Bellman-Ford algorithm; Example protocols: OSPF, RIP, BGP; Transport Layer: Connection oriented and Connectionless protocols: TCP and UDP; Details of TCP: Buffer Management, Flow Control and Congestion control; Study of TCP variants such as Reno, Tahoe, Vegas and CUBIC. Reliable transmission and Automatic Repeat Request (ARQ) protocols: Stop-and-Wait, Go-back-N, Selective Repeat; Application Layer: Study of Protocols and Applications: DNS, FTP, HTTP, and SMTP. Socket Interface and Socket programming; Advanced topics and recent trends in Computer Networks and Future Internet: Information Centric networking, Software Defined Networking (SDN), Network Function Virtualization (NFV), Internet of Things (IoT), Docker and Container Networking (CNIs), Programmable switches, OVS, and SmartNICs; Miscellaneous: Introduction to Datacenter Networks, Different RFCs; Case Studies: Linux/Unix TCP IP Stack; Network Simulators: NS-3, and Mininet; Optional: NS-2, OMNet,

courses.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Introduction to Competitive Programming
instructors: All CSE and CSE adjacent faculty members
courses.content: This is a course focused on teaching algorithms and data structures through problem solving. Students will be solving andcoding up solutions to various basic algorithmic and data structure related problems, from the lens of competitiveprogramming. Familiarizing with online judges: Understanding the various components of a problem statement, the importance of constraints, understanding hidden test files, verdicts, following the exact Input/Output prescribed in the statement, flowcharts, complexity analysis. Arrays: 1d and 2d array based problems, frequency arrays, inverse arrays, two-pointer methods, prefix sums, difference arrays, sliding windows. Sorting and Searching: Sorting algorithms, ad-hoc sorting problems, linear search, binary search, binary search on answer. Greedy: Introduction to greedy algorithms, proof techniques based on exchange arguments. Dynamic Programming (DP): Classical DP problems (0/1 Knapsack, Longest Increasing Subsequence, Edit Distance, Longest Common Subsequence, Subset Sum), Grid DPs, DP on intervals, Bitmask DP. Math for CP: Euclid's Greatest Common Divisor algorithm, Sieve of Eratosthenes, Modular arithmetic, primality testing, factorization.
courses.textbooks: There is no single textbook. Problems will be from online platforms (eg. AtCoder, Codeforces, CodeChef).; Cormen, T. H., Leiserson, C. E., Rivest, R. L., & Stein, C. (2022). Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed.). MIT Press.; Laaksonen, A. (2018). Competitive Programmer's Handbook.; Halim, S., Halim, F., & Effendy, S. (2020). Competitive Programming 4: The Lower Bound of ProgrammingContests in the 2020s (4th ed.). Lulu Press.; Skiena, S. S. (2020). The Algorithm Design Manual (3rd ed.). Springer.

courses.content: Introduction: Human-Computer Interaction (HCI); the role of an effective design(example of good and bad design) in the interaction design process; Concepts:describes major design principles associated with HCI e.g., visibility, flow,affordance, metaphors, feedback, consistency etc.; Understanding users anddata gathering: Overview of how to plan and run successful data gatheringsessions including interviews, design questionnaires and run observations; Requirements formulation: describes how to interpret results from datagathering sessions into requirements for design, explains the process andpurpose of literature reviews and goes through how to develop scenarios anduse cases; User interfaces and modalities: introduction to the many differenttypes of user interface and modalities, and how they are used; Design,prototyping and construction: explain why to prototype and how to constructprototypes (low and high-fidelity), and how to begin construction based ondesigns; Evaluation: why is evaluation important, goals, methods, case study; Data analysis: discuss the qualitative and quantitative data and describes howbest to analyze the results from data gathering sessions; Group course project(low/high-fidelity prototype) submission and presentation; Future directions:what is next for HCI and interaction design?

courses.title: History of Computing and its Applications to Domains
courses.content: Introduction: A Historical Perspective of Computer Systems - Hardware, Firmware and Software. Key inventions and Proliferations. Development of computer networks, Internet and computing platforms - the philosophy of computers, necessity and innovations, including the history and evolution of Indian ICT.Advancements: Proliferation of Computers in various domains of science and engineering, business, finance, healthcare, medicine, finance and governance. Evolution of Utility computing, Clusters, Grids, Data Centers, Cloud. Cybercrimes and Cybersecurity, Policies, Computer Ethics and Societal Impact.Future Prospects: Quantum Computing, Quantum Communication. Reflections on the lessons learnt by the History and evolution of computing.
courses.textbooks: 1. thecomputerarchive.com2. Anecdotes from the History of Modern Computing, V. Rajaraman, PHI Publishers, 2024.3. wayback.achive.orgLearning outcomes1. Students will understand the concept of Computing Systems and their evolution. 2. Students will learn about the key necessities that led to the development of different computing systems and the associated innovations.3. Students will get acquainted with the key personalities that have contributed to shape the world of computing. 4. Students will work on the Assignments and Projects that will make them think about key research areas and future computing prospects.

courses.content: Course contents:Fundamentals: Definition of Turing Machines, Time and Space, Diagonalization, Hierarchy Theorems.Poly-time (P), Nondeterministic poly-time (NP), and completeness: Definitions of P and NP, Turing reductions and many-one reductions, NP-completeness of Satisfiability (SAT).Space complexity: Logspace (L) and Nondeterministic Logspace (NL), Savitch's Theorem, logspace reductions, NL-completeness of directed graph reachability, NL=coNL (coNL indicating complement of NL), PSPACE (polynomial space).Randomness and Interaction: Bounded-error probabilistic polynomial-time (BPP) and randomized-polynomial time (RP), Polynomial Identity Testing (PIT), Valiant-Vazirani Theorem, Polynomial hierarchy (PH) and its characterizations, PSPACE (polynomial space), PSPACE-completeness of Quantified Boolean Formulas (QBF), interactive proofs (IP), Arthur-Merlin classes, IP = PSPACE.Non-uniformity: Boolean circuits, Nick's class (NC), Matching in Randomized NC (RNC), Karp-Lipton Theorem.Cryptography and Complexity: One-way functions and Pseudorandom generators.Counting: #P, Toda's theorem, The permanentCircuits: Alternating circuits of constant depth (AC0), Alternating circuits with counting (ACC0), Threshold circuits (TC0), Nick's class level 1 (NC1) etc., AC0 lower bounds.Branching programs: Barrington's Theorem, Monoid programs.Exact complexity: Exponential-time hypothesis (ETH) and Strong exponential-time hypothesis (SETH), sparsification lemma.Randomness vs Hardness: Derandomization implies circuit lower bounds.

(Textbook)Oded Goldreich, Computational Complexity: A Conceptual Perspective, 1st Edition, Cambridge University Press, 2008. (Textbook/Reference).Other References:Stasys Jukna, Boolean Function Complexity, 1st Edition, Springer, 2012.Ding-Zhu Du and Ker-I Ko, Theory of Computational Complexity, Wiley-Blackwell, 2000.Ingo Wegener, The Complexity of Boolean Functions, 1st Edition, Wiley, 1987.Heribert Vollmer, Introduction to Circuit Complexity: A Uniform Approach, 1st Edition, Springer, 1999.Oded Goldreich. Foundations of Cryptography, Volume I: Basic Tools, Cambridge University Press, 2005

courses.title: Theoretical Foundations of Machine Learning
courses.content: Concentration of measure - various concentration inequalities; PAC learning,VC dimension, Rademacher complexity, generalization bounds, sample complexity.Generalization in deep learning setting - double descent; Optimization - random projection,randomized numerical linear algebra and its applications in efficient optimization (e.g. regression,matrix factorization, second order descent); Beyond the worst-case analysis of machine learningalgorithms - algorithms for non-negative matrix factorization, tensor decomposition, sparserecovery, dictionary learning; Kernels and their applications in ML --- kernel trick, Representertheorem, Support vector machine with kernels, Green's theorem, Mercer's theorem, Bochner'stheorem; learning kernels; Subset selection and its variants --- using submodularity, usingimportance sampling (coresets). Applications in data valuation, continual learning, and inoptimization; Introduction to Gaussian Process (GP), neural network-GP equivalence, neuraltangent kernel; Learning in stochastic settings -- Introduction to online learning and multi-armedbandits; Gittin's index, Thomson sampling, adversarial bandits, contextual bandits.

courses.title: CS Theory Toolkit
courses.content: Logic: Propositions, connectives, quantifiers, rules of inference, induction, strong induction, structural induction.Combinatorics: Definition of graphs and various structures in graphs. Koenig, Hall, and Menger theorems. The TONCAS (The obvious necessary conditions are also sufficient) principle, Common proof techniques (structural induction, attacking minimal counter-examples etc.) for graph theory, algebraic techniques, algorithmic proofs (Havel-Hakimi theorem, Eulerian circuits, Pruefer codes). Advanced Topic: Parameterized algorithms: Branching algorithms and proof by cases, refining cases to increase efficiency, tree decompositions and dynamic programming (Independent set), algebraic techniques for exact algorithms (inclusion-exclusion)Probability theory: Review of basics, Expectation and variance, linearity of expectation, concentration bounds, derandomization, probabilistic methods in proofs. Advanced topic: A randomized algorithm for perfect matching.Computational models: What is computation?, motivation and definition of RAM (Random access machine) model, Algorithmic correctness and worst-case complexity. lower bounds vs upper bounds, reductions, Satisfiability and reductions to Satisfiability (Hamiltonian Cycle to Satisfiability, Vertex Cover to Satisfiability), hardness and completeness via reductions. identifying the right reductions (many-one, Turing, logspace etc.), gadget constructions (one or two advanced examples). Advanced topic: Boolean circuits, constant-depth circuit constructions, circuit reductions.

courses.title: Ethics of AI
courses.content: Algorithmic Fairness and Accountability, where students study operationalized fairness criteria, constrained optimization for fair models, and the socio-technical dimensions of bias through case studies in vision and NLP; the Interpretability and Explainability module introduces inherently interpretable models, post-hoc explanations (LIME, SHAP, Integrated Gradients, SmoothGrad), counterfactual and concept-based explanations (TCAV, Network Dissection), and connects interpretability with fairness; Mechanistic Interpretability and Representation Engineering delves into circuits, compiled transformers, and emergent reasoning in large models (e.g., Chain-of-Thought prompting, interpretability in diffusion and vision-language models); Robustness and Adversarial ML explores vulnerabilities in AI systems, the history and theory of adversarial examples, adversarial training, robustness-accuracy trade-offs, and the security of large models under adversarial or distributional shifts; Privacy and Security in AI explores data protection, membership inference,reconstruction attacks, and the theoretical and practical aspects of Differential Privacy and DP-SGD; Ethics of Large-Scale AI Systems examines the societal, legal, and governance implications of LLMs and generative models, including transparency, accountability, and global regulatory trends

courses.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Ubiquitous Computing
courses.content: The aim of this class will be to introduce the students to ubiquitous computing. We will focus on howtraditional topics of computing have evolved to support the vision of a connected, portable, and a human-centric computing environment. Thus, this course will discuss both the classic and recent view ofubiquitous computing across varied application domains.The course will include discussion of the contributions of various fields, including (given the highinterdisciplinary nature of the topics, some of the subtopics span multiple topics):● human-computer interaction: novel interfaces for interacting with the computing world, eg.gestural, haptic feedback, augmented reality, wearables, pervasive displays, etc.● embedded computing: wearable computing, edge computing, self-powered systems, intermittentpowered systems, energy harvesting, duty cycling, etc.● computer vision: activity sensing based on thermal cameras, multi-spectral imaging, etc.● distributed systems: load scheduling and balancing, edge intelligence, context and contentsummarization, cloud computing.● machine learning: applications of recent advances in processing (mostly) structured time-seriesdata towards mostly supervised and unsupervised learning problems, eg. predicting location of auser from the WiFi signal strength (regression problem); unsupervised activity recognition(clustering); edge machine learning.The students will gain practical experience in developing sensing systems in different application domains,such as:● activity recognition: sensing walking gait, activity (walking, sleeping), etc.● health sensing: sensing lung health, breathing, eating, stress, etc.● gestural interaction: novel gestural control and devices like smart rings, smart watches, andinteracting with them over varied interfaces, eg. doppler effect based web page browsing,interacting via the jaw muscle movement.● speech interaction: conversational agents, novel earables (ear wearables)● fabrication and wearables: novel fabrication technologies, novel wearables for detecting activitiesof daily life (mentioned above)● localization: localizing multiple people inside and outside home using novel sensing technologiessuch as backscatter, multi-path, etc.
courses.textbooks: Given the evolving nature of the topic, there is no prescribed textbook on the course. However, the followingis a select set of similar offerings from top universities across the world.● Physical computing course at UW offered by Jon Froehlich: https://makeabilitylab.github.io/physcomp/● Ubiquitous computing course at UW by Mayank Goel:https://www.mayankgoel.courses/teaching/ee590p-fall-2015/2● Physical computing course at NYU offered by Tom Igoe: https://itp.nyu.edu/physcomp/● Ubiquitous computing course at CMU: https://www.hcii.cmu.edu/courses/ubiquitous-computingThe course readings are based on a mix of classic and recent papers in top CS conferences such as:● ACM Ubicomp: https://ubicomp.org/● ACM Sensys: http://sensys.acm.org● ACM IPSN: https://ipsn.acm.org● ACM CHI: https://chi2021.acm.org● ACM UIST: https://uist.acm.org/uist2021/● IEEE Pervasive Computing: https://www.computer.org/csdl/magazine/pc

courses.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Usable Privacy and Security
courses.content: The aim of this course is to provide a user-focused view of security and privacy in systems. This wouldinclude topics related to secure systems and their usability analysis. We will discuss the state-of-the-artworks in the area of usable security to understand how and why usability is an important concern whendesigning systems. Some of the potential topics of interest are:● Introduction to usability with security and privacy● Usable encryption: usability of classical encryption techniques and cryptosystems● Measurement Studies: usability measurement, design of user studies, techniques - traditional andmodern, quantitative analysis techniques, statistical analysis, ethical concerns, issues and biasesin studies● Usability case studies: password management, two-factor authentication, anonymity, developer-oriented studies● User perspectives of security and privacy on the web and social media: social engineering,phishing● Data privacy regulationsThe students would perform user studies involving institute personnel for different problem statements aspart of a capstone course project.
courses.textbooks: There is no prescribed textbook for the course. We will be following research publications appearing atSOUPS, PETS, ACM CHI, Usenix Security, and similar conferences or symposiums.To understand methods for conducting usability studies, we recommend this book:● "Research Methods in Human-Computer Interaction" by Jonathan Lazar, Jinjuan Heidi Feng, HarryHochheiser.

courses.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: AI for Social Good
courses.content: Topics will span a wide array of AI domains such as but limited to:- Healthcare: AI for diagnosis, personalised medicine, public health monitoring, and remote care- Environment and Sustainability: AI in climate modelling, pollution monitoring, energy efficiency, and conservation- Disaster Resilience: AI for disaster prediction, response, and resource management- Education: Personalized learning systems, intelligent tutoring, analysis of educational outcomes- Public Safety and Security: Social engineering, AI for detecting misinformation, predictive policingThe AI technologies would include:- Supervised Learning: Applications in classification and prediction tasks (e.g., disease diagnosis, fraud detection, poverty prediction)- Unsupervised Learning: Clustering and anomaly detection for environmental monitoring, resource allocation- Semi-supervised Learning: Addressing challenges with limited labelled data in social good applications- Reinforcement Learning: Applications in resource optimization, healthcare interventions, and adaptive learning systems- Natural Language Processing (NLP): Sentiment analysis, misinformation detection, speech recognition for social applications- Computer Vision: Object detection, segmentation and classification for environmental monitoring, healthcare imaging- Deep Learning: Use of neural networks for complex social problems (e.g., medical imaging, disaster prediction)
courses.textbooks: There is no prescribed textbook for the course. We will be following research publications appearing at AAAI, IJCAI, KDD, Neurips, ICLR, and similar top conferences or symposiums.There are some related courses at top universities that would serve as a good reference- AI for Social Impact course at Harvard- Social good at Google- AI for Social Good course at Stanford- AI for social good coursera specialisation

courses.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: User Studies for Privacy & Security
courses.content: The aim of this course is to perform field studies to understand the normal users' view of security and privacy in systems. The students would perform user studies involving institute personnel for different problem statements as part of the course. There would be weekly meetings to discuss the findings and driving the survey/interviews with the users for obtaining data that can be analyzed to answer the research questions posed. The course is completely project-based, which would make the students familiar with three distinct components:1. Data Collection a. Surveys/Interviews to obtain real-world data pertaining to the research question2. Data Analysisa. Qualitative and quantitative analysis of collected datab. Choosing the right data and discarding meaningless data3. Reporting a. Report the findings in a meaningful way b. Discuss the overall trends and steps to tackle the usability/security issues if any for the general society
courses.textbooks: We will be following research publications appearing at SOUPS, PETS, ACM CHI, Usenix Security, and similar conferences or symposiums for understanding the three componentsFor conducting usability studies, we recommend this book:● "Research Methods in Human-Computer Interaction" by Jonathan Lazar, Jinjuan Heidi Feng, Harry Hochheiser.

courses.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Applications of Linear Algebra to Combinatorics
courses.content: This course explores applications of tools from linear algebra in combinatorics, geometry, algorithms, and graph theory. We will emphasize proofs and proof techniques. The goal is to use the applications as a backdrop to practice and review linear algebra skills, but also to develop a an understanding of new proof techniques and recognize how to apply them in new settings as well.

courses.title: Special Topics in Computer Science: Congestion Control
courses.content: Summary of the course:A current and concise exposition of some of the most important topics in the area of congestion control for computer networks, with special emphasis on analytical modeling of congestion control systemsAccessible coverage of the advanced topics of optimization and control theory as applied to congestion control systemsApplication of the theory to the problem of congestion control in a number of areas such as data center networks, video streaming, high-speed links, and broadband wireless networks.Course contents:Introduction to Congestion Control: Chiu-Jain result on the optimality of AIMD control, Fundamental congestion control algorithms such TCP Reno, TCP Vegas and RED based Active Queue Management.Congestion Control Models: Models using fluid approximations, Models using stochastic theory, "Square Root" formula for throughput of TCP, Generalization to AIMD congestion control schemes, Optimization Theory and Control Theory, Nyquist Criterion.Congestion Control in Broadband Wireless and High Speed Networks: TCP over wireless links, high-speed networks with long latencies. Congestion Control in Data Center Networks Congestion Control in Video Delivery: HTTP Adaptive Streaming (HAS) Congestion Control in Ethernet Networks: IEEE802.1Qau or Quantum Congestion Notification (QCN) Emerging Topics: Use of Machine Learning to design advanced control algorithms.

courses.title: Probabilistic Machine Learning
courses.content: Probability refresher: Probability theory, discrete distributions, continuous distributions, joint probability distributions, sampling from different distributions (e.g. using Box-Muller transform), uncertainty modelling, information theoretic concepts: (KL-divergence, entropy); Bayesian concept learning: Likelihood, Prior, Posterior, Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE), Maximum A-Posteriori estimation (MAP), Full Bayesian Estimation with Conjugate Priors (Beta-Bernoulli model for the coin toss example, Normal-Normal model for estimating parameters of Normal distribution); Bayesian supervised methods: Regression: Bayesian linear regression, Robust linear regression via alternative likelihood (e.g. Laplace, Student-T); Classification: Bayesian Logistic regression, Bayesian Naive Bayes; Latent Variable modelling: Gaussian Mixture Model, Probabilistic principal component analysis (PPCA); Approximate Inference: Sampling based strategies: Rejection sampling, Importance sampling, Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), Metropolis Hastings, Gibbs sampling, No U-Turn sampler (NUTS); Variational inference: Mean field approach, Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO), Reparameterization trick, Stochastic Variational Inference, Automatic Differentiation Variational Inference (ADVI); Gaussian Processes (GP): Multivariate Normal distribution and its properties, Kernels, GP regression, GP classification, Approximate Inference and Sparse GPs; Bayesian Optimization (BO) and Active Learning (AL): AL - Query by committee, Uncertainty sampling, Expected model change, BO - Acquisition functions, GP based BO, Random Forests based BO; Probabilistic Deep Learning: MC Dropout, Deep Ensembles, Bayesian neural networks (BNNs), Deep GPs.

courses.textbooks: The objective of this short course is to introduce the theoretical foundations of state estimation. Specifically, we will consider the following general framework: given a set of observations, we make inferences about some underlying phenomenon of interest. This will include choosing between a fixed set of possible scenarios (hypothesis) as well as estimating a particular parameter of interest. In both cases, we will try to optimize some form of cost function. With the focus on the latter problem (parameter estimation), this course will equip the students with the theoretical foundations necessary to address numerous practical problems that span all areas of engineering, computer science and even management.
